title: The Rainy Village
story_beat: We meet Luna and her grey, rain-soaked village. While other children stay inside, Luna ventures out — convinced the rain is trying to tell her something.

techniques.name: sensory_detail
techniques.reason: The opening pages need rich sensory language to immerse the reader in the sound and feel of constant rain.

techniques.name: character_voice
techniques.reason: Luna's curiosity and determination must come through immediately — she's different from the other children.

techniques.name: show_dont_tell
techniques.reason: Quick reminder to show Luna's bravery through actions (pulling on boots, splashing out) rather than stating it.

wonder_prompt: Think about a time you noticed something nobody else did — a sound, a colour, a feeling. What made you stop and pay attention when everyone else walked by?
wonder_example: "Max wore his wolf suit and made mischief of one kind and another, so his mother called him 'WILD THING!' and Max said 'I'LL EAT YOU UP!' so he was sent to bed without eating anything." — Maurice Sendak, *Where the Wild Things Are*. Notice how Sendak shows Max's wild personality through his actions and words, not by telling us he's wild.
build_instructions: It's time to set the scene! Picture Luna's village on a grey, rainy morning. The rain never stops here — it patters on rooftops, drums on windows, turns puddles into tiny seas. Now introduce Luna: a girl who does something nobody else does. She goes outside. Write pages 1-3, bringing the rain to life with sounds and textures, and showing us who Luna is through what she does and says.
page_prompts: Set the scene: the village, the endless rain, the grey world. Use at least three different sounds the rain makes.
Show the contrast: other children stay inside, but Luna pulls on her red boots and heads out. What does this tell us about her?
Luna's grandmother asks why she goes out in the rain. Write their exchange. Luna's answer should hint at the mystery to come.
reflect_preview: Next session, Luna will discover something extraordinary in a puddle — a golden seed that hums. What could it mean? And what happens when she plants it in the grey mud?
